section.title: The Grain Offering
section.sectionRationale: The second of the five great offerings, the grain offering is bloodless, made of fine flour, oil, and frankincense. It continues the sacrificial law collection begun in chapter one, showing that worship of God involves the fruit of ordinary labor as well as the blood of animals.
section.thesis: The grain offering, seasoned with salt and free of leaven or honey, teaches Israel to bring the best of their labor to God as a memorial offering, most holy, a portion given wholly to Him and to His priests.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the offering of raw flour and oil; the offering baked, on a griddle, or in a pan; the prohibitions of leaven and honey and the requirement of salt; and the offering of firstfruits.

units.label: Flour, oil, and frankincense
units.note: a memorial portion burned to the LORD

verses.text: “When anyone brings a {{minchah|grain offering}} to the LORD, his offering must consist of fine flour. He is to pour olive oil on it, put frankincense on it,

and bring it to Aaron's sons the priests. The priest shall take a handful of the flour and oil, together with all the frankincense, and burn this as a memorial portion on the altar, an offering made by fire, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
The remainder of the grain offering shall belong to Aaron and his sons; it is a {{qodeshQodashim|most holy}} part of the offerings made by fire to the LORD.

units.label: Baked, on a griddle, or in a pan
units.note: three prepared forms of the same offering

verses.text: Now if you bring an offering of grain baked in an oven, it must consist of fine flour, either unleavened cakes mixed with oil or unleavened wafers coated with oil.
If your offering is a grain offering prepared on a griddle, it must be unleavened bread made of fine flour mixed with oil.
Crumble it and pour oil on it; it is a grain offering.
If your offering is a grain offering cooked in a pan, it must consist of fine flour with oil.
When you bring to the LORD the grain offering made in any of these ways, it is to be presented to the priest, and he shall take it to the altar.
The priest is to remove the memorial portion from the grain offering and burn it on the altar as an offering made by fire, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
But the remainder of the grain offering shall belong to Aaron and his sons; it is a most holy part of the offerings made by fire to the LORD.

units.label: No leaven, no honey, only salt

verses.text: No grain offering that you present to the LORD may be made with leaven, for you are not to burn any leaven or honey as an offering made by fire to the LORD.
You may bring them to the LORD as an offering of firstfruits, but they are not to be offered on the altar as a pleasing aroma.

And you shall season each of your grain offerings with {{melach|salt}}. You must not leave the salt of the covenant of your God out of your grain offering; you are to add salt to each of your offerings.
If you bring a grain offering of firstfruits to the LORD, you shall offer crushed heads of new grain roasted on the fire.
And you are to put oil and frankincense on it; it is a grain offering.
The priest shall then burn the memorial portion of the crushed grain and the oil, together with all its frankincense, as an offering made by fire to the LORD.

minchah.gloss: A grain offering or gift, sometimes used of a tribute given to a superior. This bloodless offering of flour, oil, and frankincense honors God with the fruit of ordinary daily labor, not only with animal sacrifice.

qodeshQodashim.gloss: Most holy, or holy of holies, the highest degree of consecration. What remains of the grain offering is set at this level, meaning only priests could eat it, and only in a holy place.

melach.gloss: The salt of the covenant. Salt preserves and does not decay, so its required presence in every grain offering pictures the permanence and faithfulness of God's covenant with His people, compare Numbers 18:19 and 2 Chronicles 13:5.

basic.context: After the burnt offering, God gives instructions for the grain offering, a bloodless gift of fine flour, oil, and frankincense. Unlike the burnt offering, most of it belonged to the priests to eat, though a memorial portion was always burned to God. It could be raw, baked, griddled, or pan cooked, but it always had to include salt and never leaven or honey.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes an actual old covenant ritual for Israel, not a pattern Christians are to reenact. Its abiding truth is that God is honored not only through costly blood sacrifice but through the ordinary produce of daily work, offered without corruption and seasoned with covenant faithfulness.
basic.originalLanguages: Minchah, grain offering, can also mean tribute or gift, showing that this offering honors God as a superior worthy of gifts from His people's labor. The remainder is qodesh qodashim, most holy, and every offering required melach berit, the salt of the covenant, a symbol of God's unbreakable faithfulness.
basic.typology: The fine, unleavened flour points toward the sinless perfection of Christ, since leaven throughout Scripture often pictures corruption or sin working through a whole batch. The salt of the covenant anticipates the permanence of the new covenant sealed in Christ's blood, a covenant that, unlike salt's endurance, will never decay.
basic.christ: Christ is the true bread from heaven, John 6:35, offered without the leaven of sin, 1 Peter 2:22. As the grain offering was crushed and burned, so Christ was crushed for our iniquities, Isaiah 53:5, His life given as a fragrant offering to God, Ephesians 5:2.
basic.god: God delights to receive not only blood sacrifice but the honest fruit of daily labor, showing that worship touches every part of ordinary life, not merely the dramatic or costly. His covenant faithfulness, pictured in salt that never decays, means His promises to His people never spoil or fail.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 5:2 calls Christ's self giving a fragrant offering to God. 1 Corinthians 5:6-8 warns that a little leaven leavens the whole batch, urging believers to keep the feast without the leaven of malice. Numbers 18:19 calls God's covenant with the priesthood a covenant of salt.
basic.oneStory: The grain offering teaches Israel that worship includes the ordinary fruit of daily labor, offered without corruption and sealed by the enduring salt of the covenant. It anticipates Christ, the unleavened bread of life, crushed and offered as a pleasing aroma so that God's covenant faithfulness to His people would never fail.

context.literary: The grain offering follows immediately after the burnt offering in chapter one, and the two are often paired in later practice, the grain offering accompanying the daily burnt offerings, compare Exodus 29:38-42.
context.historical: For an agricultural people entering the land, offering the fruit of the field acknowledged that the harvest itself belonged to the LORD, who had given the land and its produce in the first place.
context.canonical: The grain offering's prohibition of leaven and requirement of salt become recurring symbols across Scripture, leaven for corrupting influence and salt for enduring covenant faithfulness, both picked up directly by Jesus and Paul in the New Testament.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To instruct Israel in offering the produce of their labor to God in a form that excludes corruption, leaven and honey, and includes covenant faithfulness, salt, teaching that worship is not limited to blood sacrifice.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This prescribes a specific ritual for Israel's old covenant worship; it is not a pattern for Christian offerings today. The abiding principle is that all of life, including ordinary labor, is meant to be offered to God without corruption and in covenant faithfulness.

debates.question: Why are leaven and honey excluded from offerings made by fire, yet permitted as firstfruits, verse 12?

views.label: Leaven and honey ferment or spoil quickly
views.case: Both substances undergo processes of corruption or rapid decay, unsuitable for an offering meant to picture purity and endurance before a holy God.

views.label: A distinction between the altar offering and general gifts
views.case: Verse 12 allows leaven and honey to be brought to the LORD as firstfruits generally, but not placed on the altar as a fire offering, suggesting the restriction concerns nearness to the altar specifically rather than all use of these foods in worship.

debates.bearing: Either way, the text draws a careful line around what may ascend on the altar itself, reserving that space for what pictures purity and lasting faithfulness.

study.originalLanguages: Minchah names both the grain offering and a tribute gift, showing worship as homage brought to a great King. The remainder given to the priests is qodesh qodashim, most holy, and every grain offering required melach berit, the salt of the covenant, a phrase that ties this humble offering of flour to God's unbreakable promises to His people.

typology.fulfillment: The sinless perfection of Christ, who knew no sin, 2 Corinthians 5:21, 1 Peter 2:22.
Christ, crushed for our iniquities, whose suffering brought us peace, Isaiah 53:5.

typology.type: The salt of the covenant, never to be omitted
typology.fulfillment: The enduring, unbreakable new covenant sealed in Christ's blood, Luke 22:20.

study.christ: Jesus calls Himself the bread of life come down from heaven, John 6:35, and Paul describes His self giving on the cross as a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God, Ephesians 5:2, language drawn directly from the grain and burnt offerings. Where the grain offering pictured purity through the absence of leaven, Christ actually lived without sin, and where it pictured covenant faithfulness through salt, He established the new covenant in His own blood.
study.god: God receives worship not only in the dramatic shedding of blood but in the ordinary produce of the field and the daily bread of His people's labor, showing that no part of life is outside His claim. His insistence on the salt of the covenant in every grain offering reveals a God whose promises endure and do not spoil, unlike so much that decays in this world.

crossReferences.note: Christ gave Himself up for us as a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God, language echoing the grain and burnt offerings.
A little leaven leavens the whole batch; believers are called to celebrate the feast without the leaven of malice and evil.
God's provision for the priesthood is called a covenant of salt forever, an enduring and unbreakable covenant, echoing the salt required here.

study.oneStory: The grain offering shows that worship of God reaches into ordinary daily labor, the flour and oil of everyday life, offered without corruption and marked by the enduring salt of the covenant. That pattern anticipates Christ, the true bread of life, sinless and crushed for our sake, whose offering seals a covenant that, unlike anything in this world, will never spoil or fail.

quiz.q: What three ingredients make up the basic grain offering?

options.text: Fine flour, olive oil, and frankincense.
options.note: Verse 1 lists these three elements as the core of the grain offering.
Honey is explicitly forbidden in the fire offering, verse 11; this is not the correct combination.
Salt is required, but yeast, leaven, is explicitly forbidden, and water is not named as an ingredient.
Verse 1 gives a specific and detailed list of ingredients.

quiz.q: What happens to the remainder of the grain offering after the memorial portion is burned?

options.text: It belongs to Aaron and his sons as a most holy portion.
options.note: Verses 3 and 10 both state this explicitly.

options.text: It is thrown away as unclean.
options.note: The remainder is not discarded; it is a most holy portion given to the priests.

options.text: It is returned to the worshiper to eat at home.
options.note: The remainder belongs to the priests, not back to the one who brought the offering.

options.text: The whole offering is always burned completely, with nothing left over.
options.note: Only a memorial handful is burned; the remainder belongs to the priests.

quiz.q: What two things must never be included in a grain offering burned on the altar?

options.note: Verse 11 explicitly forbids both leaven and honey in offerings made by fire.
These are required ingredients of the grain offering, not forbidden ones.
Salt is required, verse 13, and fine flour is the basic ingredient; neither is forbidden.
Verse 11 explicitly names two forbidden ingredients.

quiz.q: What must be added to every grain offering, described as 'the salt of the covenant of your God'?

options.note: Verse 13 requires salt in every grain offering, calling it the salt of the covenant.
Wine is not mentioned as a required addition to the grain offering in this chapter.
Honey is explicitly forbidden, the opposite of what is required.
Verse 13 explicitly requires salt to be added.

quiz.q: How is the firstfruits grain offering, verses 14 to 16, to be prepared?

options.text: As crushed heads of new grain, roasted on the fire, with oil and frankincense added.
options.note: Verses 14 to 15 describe exactly this preparation.

options.text: As fine flour only, with no oil or frankincense.
options.note: Oil and frankincense are explicitly added, verse 15.

options.text: Mixed with leaven for a festive loaf.
options.note: Leaven remains forbidden even for the firstfruits offering placed on the altar.
Verses 14 to 16 give a detailed, specific preparation.

questions: The grain offering brought the ordinary produce of daily labor to God. What part of your everyday work or income have you never thought to offer to Him?
Leaven and honey, associated with corruption and decay, were kept out of the offering. What corrupting influence do you need to remove before you can worship God with an undivided heart?
Salt, the sign of the covenant, was never to be left out. Where do you need reminding that God's covenant faithfulness to you does not decay or run out?
Most of the grain offering fed the priests who served at the altar. How well do you support those who give their lives to serving God's people?
Christ is the bread of life, offered without the leaven of sin. What would it look like this week to feed on Him rather than on the corrupting things you are tempted to consume instead?

translationNotes.note: Grain offering translates minchah, a word that elsewhere in the Old Testament describes a tribute or gift brought to a king or superior, framing this bloodless offering as homage to God as Israel's true Sovereign.
Leaven, se'or, and honey, devash, were likely excluded from the altar because both ferment or spoil readily, unsuited to an offering meant to picture purity and permanence before God.
The salt of the covenant of your God echoes ancient Near Eastern treaty language, where salt symbolized a binding, enduring agreement, compare Numbers 18:19 and 2 Chronicles 13:5.
